title: Optimizing Quantum Entanglement Preservation in a Qubit Qubit System with Dzyaloshinskii Moriya Interaction under Noisy Magnetic Fields via Feedback Control
abstract: Quantum entanglement is a key resource for quantum information processing and sensing, but it is severely degraded by environmental noise. We extend the previous study by Moosavi Khansari and Kazemi Hasanvand [27] of entanglement dynamics in a qubit qubit system with Dzyaloshinskii Moriya (DM) interaction and static magnetic fields to the realistic case of time varying, stochastic magnetic fields. We derive a stochastic Lindblad master equation and simulate quantum trajectories to quantify the negativity under colored noise. We then design a proportional integral feedback protocol that dynamically adjusts the DM interaction strength D_z (t) to maintain negativity near a target value. The feedback stabilized state is used as a probe for quantum metrology: we compute the quantum Fisher information (QFI) for estimating an unknown static field B_0. Our simulations show that feedback increases the time averaged negativity from 0.21 to 0.42 for α=1 at noise amplitude σ=0.5, leading to a factor 2.4 improvement in sensitivity over the classical shot noise limit. This work provides a practical route to protect entanglement in noisy environments and enhances quantum sensing performance.
summary: This paper proposes a real-time feedback control method to protect entanglement in a two-qubit system subject to noisy magnetic fields. By dynamically adjusting the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction, the researchers significantly stabilize negativity and enhance the sensitivity of quantum magnetometry. The approach provides a robust way to maintain quantum resources in realistic, fluctuating environments.
main_problem: The degradation of quantum entanglement in two-qubit systems due to time-varying, stochastic magnetic fields and the challenge of preserving this resource for quantum sensing.
main_result: A Proportional-Integral (PI) feedback control protocol that dynamically adjusts the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction strength to stabilize entanglement, doubling time-averaged negativity and providing a 2.4-fold improvement in metrological sensitivity over the classical limit.
method: The study uses a stochastic Lindblad master equation and quantum trajectory simulations (Monte Carlo wavefunction method) with an Euler-Maruyama scheme to model Ornstein-Uhlenbeck colored noise.
model_or_system: A two-qubit system (XXX and XYZ Heisenberg models) interacting via Heisenberg exchange and Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) interaction, subject to stochastic magnetic fields and Lindblad-type decoherence (amplitude damping and dephasing).
key_observables: Negativity (as a measure of entanglement), Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) for field estimation, and the two-qubit correlation <sigma_a^z sigma_b^z> used as a proxy for negativity.
important_parameters: Noise amplitude (sigma), noise correlation time (tau_c), DM interaction strength (D_z), anisotropy parameter (alpha), and the target negativity (N_target = 0.4).
main_assumptions: The noise is modeled as an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process (colored noise); the feedback control relies on an experimentally accessible proxy observable.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the calibration between negativity and the proxy observable; Figure 2 shows negativity decay under increasing noise; Figure 3 demonstrates stabilization via PI feedback; Figure 4 shows average negativity vs. initial DM strength; Figure 5 shows performance in the XYZ model.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of noise-induced decoherence, defines the Heisenberg-DM Hamiltonian and stochastic noise model, presents the PI feedback control design, validates the proxy observable, and evaluates the resulting entanglement stabilization and metrological sensitivity gains.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum metrology as it demonstrates a practical control strategy (PI feedback) to mitigate the effects of non-Markovian-like colored noise and directly links entanglement preservation to enhanced sensing precision.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 3: (Color online) Same as Fig. 2 but with feedback control (PI gains 𝑘𝑝= 0.5, 𝑘𝑖= 0.1). Dashed

Figure 4 summarizes the time-averaged negativity 𝑁‾ as a function of the initial DM strength 𝐷𝑧(0) for 𝛼= 1,2,3 and 𝜎= 0.5, with feedback ON (solid) and OFF (dashed). Without feedback, 𝑁‾ decreases with 𝛼 and is only weakly dependent on 𝐷𝑧(0). With feedback, 𝑁‾ is much higher and shows a broad optimum around 𝐷𝑧(0) = 0.8 −1.2. The best performance is for 𝛼= 1 (the most entangled initial state), reaching 𝑁‾ ≈0.45.
